reason: MISSING DISTRIBUTIONAL TRANSFORMATION LAW DEFINITION: The proof claims T'^{־±־²}(x') = ־›^־±_־³ ־›^־²_־´ T^{־³־´}(x), but never defines what T'^{־±־²} IS as a distribution. A rigorous proof must: (a) define the transformed distribution T'^{־±־²} by its action on test functions ג€” either as T'^{־±־²} acting on ֿ† via <T'^{־±־²}, ֿ†> = ־£_j גˆ« p'^־±_j(ֿ„) ־³ּ‡'^־²_j(ֿ„) ֿ†(־³'_j(ֿ„)) dֿ„ where ־³'=־›־³ and p'=־›p, or via the pullback <T'^{־±־²}, ֿ†> = <T^{־³־´}, ֿ†גˆ˜־›> ־›^־±_־³ ־›^־²_־´; (b) then PROVE these two definitions coincide. The current proof says the integrand 'consists of' vectors and scalars, concluding 'the transformation law follows.' This is the INDEX-COUNTING ARGUMENT from physics texts, not a distributional proof. In the distributional framework, the precise claim is: for T' defined using transformed worldline ־³'=־›־³ and transformed momentum p'=־›p, one has <T'^{־±־²}, ֿ†> = ־›^־±_־³ ־›^־²_־´ <T^{־³־´}, ֿ†גˆ˜־›^{-1}> for all test functions ֿ†. This requires showing: (i) ֿ†(־³'(ֿ„)) = ֿ†(־›־³(ֿ„)) = (ֿ†גˆ˜־›)(־³(ֿ„)), so the composition ֿ†גˆ˜־› acts as the 'old' test function; (ii) p'^־± ־³ּ‡'^־² = ־›^־±_־³ p^־³ ֲ· ־›^־²_־´ ־³ּ‡^־´ factoring out the ־› matrices; (iii) dֿ„ is invariant. None of these steps appears in the proof.
